intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: A vertical sidebar for experiment categories paired with spacious card layouts in the main area will feel both organized and approachable for health tracking
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers persistent navigation and visual breathing room over compact layouts
facade.rejectImplies: User may favor tab-based navigation, denser information displays, or list-based views

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments prefer clean, unfussy data visualization over colorful graphs
facade.label: Minimal line charts
facade.acceptImplies: Build with spare line graphs, whitespace, no gradients or excessive color coding
facade.rejectImplies: Users may want richer visual feedback—consider cards with icons, color zones, or more ornamental design elements

facade.hypothesis: Users building health experiments want a dashboard voice that celebrates progress and normalizes iteration, not clinical detachment
facade.label: Encouraging tone
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into warm, celebratory language. Use 'nice work,' 'let's explore,' 'you're learning.' Humanize the feedback loop.
facade.rejectImplies: Favor neutral, factual tone. Use 'data recorded,' 'metric updated,' 'analysis complete.' Remove cheerleading—let the data speak.

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard feels more approachable and sustainable when charts use rounded shapes, muted colors, and gentle animations rather than sharp lines and high-contrast visualizations
facade.label: Soft data graphs
facade.acceptImplies: The interface should prioritize emotional comfort and human-centered design over clinical precision or data density
facade.rejectImplies: The interface should lean into clarity and directness, using conventional chart styles that prioritize information hierarchy and quick scanning

evidence.hypothesis: A vertical sidebar for experiment categories paired with spacious card layouts in the main area will feel both organized and approachable for health tracking
evidence.implication: User prefers persistent navigation and visual breathing room over compact layouts

facade.hypothesis: For health experiments, users prefer seeing one key number per card (e.g., 'Weight: 187 lbs') over dense tables that show multiple variables at once
facade.axisTargeted: granularity & cognitive load — whether the dashboard shows data atomized into single values or consolidated into rich tables
facade.label: Single metric cards
facade.acceptImplies: User leans toward card-based layouts with one metric per card. Design should emphasize visual hierarchy, whitespace, and quick glanceable facts.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants more data density — multiple metrics visible at once per section. Consider table rows, grouped columns, or consolidated multi-variable cards.

facade.hypothesis: Animated state changes between dashboard views feel more satisfying and progress-tracking moments
facade.axisTargeted: animated fluid motion vs instant static updates
facade.label: Smooth transitions
facade.acceptImplies: User expects smooth transitions, fades, and animated charts when viewing health data over time; prefer motion to signal progression
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers instant loading and snappy clicks with minimal animation; motion feels slow or distracting in a health tracking context

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking experience
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking experience
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: A tab-based navigation system at the top might feel lighter and less visually committed than a persistent sidebar, allowing users to focus on one experiment at a time rather than always seeing the full category structure
facade.label: Tabs over sidebar
facade.acceptImplies: Users prefer sequential, focused views over always-visible hierarchies; a tab-based layout scales well as experiments grow
facade.rejectImplies: The sidebar+cards approach remains the stronger anchor; persistent category visibility and spatial organization matter more than tab simplicity
